title: Dog Hyperactive After ADHD Medication: Emergency Guide
meta_desc: If your dog ingested ADHD medication and is hyperactive, it is a medical emergency. Learn about stimulant toxicity and the urgent steps to take.
content_html:
What is ADHD Medication Toxicity?

ADHD medications, such as Adderall, Ritalin, and Vyvanse, contain stimulants like amphetamines or methylphenidate. While these help humans focus, they are highly toxic to dogs. Ingestion causes a massive surge in the central nervous system, leading to dangerous levels of hyperactivity, increased heart rate, and elevated blood pressure.

Is it an Emergency? (Urgency Level: High)

Yes, this is a life-threatening veterinary emergency. If your dog is showing signs of hyperactivity, pacing, or agitation after ingesting these pills, you must contact a veterinarian or an animal poison control center immediately. Without treatment, stimulant toxicity can lead to tremors, seizures, heart failure, or even death.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a clear photo of the medication bottle and the label is one of the most helpful things you can do for the veterinary team. A photo allows the vet to see the exact milligram dosage, the specific active ingredients, and whether the medication is immediate-release or extended-release (XR). This information is critical for calculating the toxicity level and determining the correct treatment protocol for your dog.

faqs.question: Can I wait to see if the symptoms go away on their own?
faqs.answer: No. Stimulant toxicity progresses rapidly and can cause permanent organ damage or death. Immediate medical intervention is required to stabilize the dog's heart rate and temperature.

faqs.question: How much ADHD medication is toxic to a dog?
faqs.answer: There is no safe dose of human ADHD medication for dogs. Even a single pill can be fatal depending on the size of the dog and the strength of the medication.

faqs.question: What are the common signs of stimulant poisoning?
faqs.answer: Common signs include extreme hyperactivity, dilated pupils, heavy panting, tremors, high body temperature, and a racing heartbeat.
